data.title: USMV: If It Will Ever Outperform, Now Is The Time
data.description:
Exactly one year ago, I warned readers that the iShares Edge MSCI Min Vol USA ETF (USMV) was "not as safe as [they] may think". Since then, the low volatility ETF returned a meager 5% against the S&P 500's (SPY) much healthier 23%. To make matters worse, as the chart below depicts, the fund has undergone drawdowns of similar magnitude through the recent Middle East crisis, despite having weathered last year's Liberation Day better - although arguably not so much so as to justify the severe underperformance.

That said, my stance towards USMV has changed a bit. I think that, while this is not how I would play defense with my portfolio, the ETF may now have its best chance of outperforming the broad market indices in this decade.

On USMV

As I discussed last year, the iShares Edge MSCI Min Vol USA ETF is an inexpensive (annual fee of 15 bps) instrument that offers investors very diversified exposure (170-plus stocks) to lower-volatility large- and mid-cap stocks. As of March 2026, the fund was invested as follows:

Investors who choose to own this fund need to be comfortable not only with the low expected volatility, which could translate to lower returns as well (although not necessarily), but with a few other of USMV's key characteristics. For instance, the ETF invests only about 15% in the top ten holdings, suggesting much deeper diversification than most equity funds.

Also, technology is much less of a key holding relative to SPY, let alone the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ), with sector allocation being much more evenly distributed across the top three (see second pie chart above). Tactically, don't expect USMV to fare particularly well in a scenario of rising crude oil prices, since its exposure to energy is only 3.4% of the total assets - it's Exxon (XOM) and not much more.

From a historical performance perspective, USMV has produced cumulative gains of about 390% since the October 2011 inception against the S&P 500's 600%-plus. Virtually all the underperformance vs. the benchmark has come since the pandemic drawdown of March 2020, as the chart below depicts.

I write about USMV on a day that the S&P 500 is down more than 1% while the low volatility ETF is up minimally. Therefore, there is clearly an element of defensiveness to this fund that should not be ignored (lower historical volatility vs. the S&P 500 by 250 bps, tamer maximum drawdown by nearly five percentage points), and that may even be appreciated by many who have not minded the much lower historical returns.

Why the sudden optimism

I hope to see the S&P 500 reclaim all-time highs soon, weathering the geopolitical pressures, the stagflation worries, and the fears about overvaluation of stocks (especially tech). Should this be the case, I would not be surprised to see the broad index produce gains in the double digits again over the next 12 months, while USMV would likely trail it.

But should the bearish narrative prove more accurate instead, USMV is more likely to beat the (negative) returns of the S&P 500 in the foreseeable future due to the following reasons:

  • Among the sectors with the lowest correlation with the S&P 500, consumer staples (XLP) and utilities (XLU) rank high at correlation coefficients of less than 0.7 and 0.6, respectively. USMV allocates nearly one-fifth of its assets to these two sectors vs. less than 9% in the case of the S&P 500.
  • Since reaching the bottom of the COVID-19 gutter, the tech sector has, once again, topped the performance of the equities market at large (see below). A reversal to the mean would not be out of the question, especially if (1) stagflation fears cause interest rates to remain high (higher growth stocks, which include many within the technology space, tend to be the most sensitive to this macro factor); and/or (2) geopolitical tensions have a negative impact on supply chains.

I still prefer multi-asset diversification

Despite being a bit more constructive towards USMV than I was this time last year, I continue to have very different thoughts about how to best play defense in the current market environment. For instance, I think that blending stocks with treasuries and even trend following strategies makes the most sense if the goal is to reduce drawdowns while producing superior risk-adjusted performance. USMV would be a lower-shelf alternative, in my view.

data.title: A Wider Path To Growth
data.description:
Over the past several years, equity market performance has been defined by concentration. A narrow cohort of mega-cap, technology-oriented companies has driven a disproportionate share of index returns, powered by superior earnings growth.

These businesses - built on software, cloud computing, digital advertising, e-commerce, and data infrastructure - scaled with extraordinary efficiency. And their asset-light models, global reach, and high incremental margins allowed them to compound capital at exceptionally high rates.

As digital transformation accelerated, economic gains accrued to a relatively small group of companies that controlled core layers of the digital ecosystem. Equity index construction then amplified this effect: as these firms grew, their weight in capitalization-weighted benchmarks increased, reinforcing their influence on overall market performance.

Markets, however, are dynamic. Leadership regimes can persist for long periods, but they do not last indefinitely. Signs have been emerging that growth rates across the market are beginning to converge. So, while mega-cap leaders remain compelling, we believe the relative gap in earnings acceleration between them and the broader market appears to be narrowing.

From Digital Dominance to Physical Expansion

We believe a key driver of this shift is the evolving nature of growth itself. The previous cycle was dominated by IP-driven expansion, but we believe the next phase of growth may be more physically grounded and capital-intensive, shaped by power generation, transmission networks, manufacturing facilities, robotics, and advanced compute infrastructure.

This distinction matters because physical investment distributes economic benefits differently - activating complex supply chains.

For example, constructing a new data center requires land development, electrical equipment, transformers, cooling systems, construction services, steel, semiconductors, and grid interconnections. In this scenario, each incremental dollar of investment circulates across a broad ecosystem of suppliers, contractors, and service providers.

Five Themes Driving the Next Investment Cycle

We believe small- and mid-cap companies - often embedded deep within larger companies’ supply chains - may have the opportunity to capture incremental demand that was less accessible during the platform-dominated era. Below are five thematic currents we believe underpin this evolution.

Theme 1: Power and Energy Infrastructure

The buildout of power and energy infrastructure has become a defining priority as AI adoption accelerates electricity demand. Data centers, particularly those optimized for AI training and inference, require substantial and reliable power. We believe meeting this demand will involve not only utilities and power producers but also equipment manufacturers and engineering firms.

Theme 2: Grid Modernization

Grid modernization and reliability upgrades appear to be becoming structural imperatives as well. As economies electrify and integrate new energy sources, infrastructure that was built decades ago stands to be upgraded to ensure reliability and resilience. This process will likely require sustained investment in advanced transmission systems, smart grid technologies, and distributed energy integration to support long-term system stability and growth.

Theme 3: The Expansion of AI Infrastructure

We believe the universe of AI beneficiaries is broader than it first appeared. The expansion of compute infrastructure depends on an extensive ecosystem, inclusive of semiconductor fabrication, specialty materials, networking equipment, cooling technologies, and construction.

Over time, AI-driven productivity gains and large-scale infrastructure investment could cascade across industrial automation, logistics, energy, real estate, and other capital-intensive sectors, broadening the economic impact well beyond the platform companies that deploy these systems.

Theme 4: Robotics and Physical AI

Advances in robotics and automation are also extending the reach of AI into the physical world. What began as a digital transformation is increasingly influencing factories, warehouses, and logistics systems. This convergence of AI-powered software and machinery could expand the range of beneficiaries to include industrial automation companies, precision manufacturers, and engineering specialists, for instance.

Theme 5: National Security

At the same time, national security considerations seem to be reshaping fiscal priorities. Heightened geopolitical tensions have prompted many governments to increase defense spending and expand beyond traditional hardware to include autonomous systems, cyber capabilities, and secure communications. This shift could direct capital toward a broader industrial base, including manufacturers, component suppliers, and specialized technology providers.

A Broader Foundation for Market Leadership

Taken together, we believe these developments suggest that market leadership may be poised to broaden as growth opportunities diffuse across sectors and capitalizations.

Small- and mid-cap companies, particularly those tied to infrastructure and capital goods spending, may find themselves supported by durable, multiyear investment cycles.

However, this does not imply the obsolescence of mega-cap leaders, which remain central to innovation. Rather, it points toward a more balanced environment - one in which leadership is less narrowly defined, and growth contributions are more evenly distributed.

Traditionally cyclical industries - long characterized by earnings volatility and sensitivity to economic swings - could begin to assume features of structural growth industries if underpinned by factors such as durable policy support, secular growth trends, and national strategic priorities.

Investment Implications

We believe this evolving backdrop argues for a reassessment of concentration risk and opportunity breadth. Periods of narrow leadership can often reward passive exposure to capitalization-weighted indices, but a market regime characterized by broadening growth may reward a more selective approach that looks beyond the largest companies.

Diversification, in this context, becomes more than a risk management tool. We believe it becomes a way of capturing multiple engines of expansion.

Looking ahead, the expected transition to better market breadth will likely not occur in a single quarter, nor do we believe it will unfold in a straight line. As growth drivers become more diffuse, the architecture of stock market returns may shift accordingly.

For investors willing to look beyond the familiar leaders of the past decade, we believe the next cycle may offer wider, more balanced investment opportunities than markets have provided in recent years.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: How Does Debt Move Through The Global Financial System?
data.description:
By Sara Carter

Global sovereign debt issuance – when a government issues bonds to finance their public spending – is expected to be high again in 2026, following a record 2025. As this issuance wave continues, and investor demand for government debt remains strong, it’s worth considering how this debt moves throughout the global financial system and its impact on the key parties involved.

Connecting Through Repo Markets

Repo markets are the plumbing behind sovereign debt distribution, ensuring bonds can be financed, hedged and reused as collateral. The journey of sovereign debt begins with government auctions and syndications, where primary dealers (large banks) and wider market participants, such as buy-side institutions, purchase bonds, which are generally offered at a discount. Corporate bonds are similar, however, issued by a firm.

To avoid utilizing the bank's own capital and balance sheet, dealers "repo" the bonds – selling them to cash-rich institutions like money market funds with an agreement to buy them back at a specified future date (terms vary per market).

While the cash bond market is an outright purchase or sale, the repo market is treated as a collateralized loan, meaning banks have to manage the associated credit risk of the underlying bond and counterparty during the term of the trade.

Government and corporate bonds, in addition to the risk positioning in outright markets, serve as collateral to finance longs/cover shorts for market participants and are utilized in margin calls. This plumbing further assists the breadth of market participants to cash reinvest, increase leverage, enhance returns and support market liquidity. The plumbing is sensitive to some friction:

1. Balance Sheet Pressure

Balance sheet pressure arises when capital requirements, deriving from the implementation of Basel Standards, such as the Leverage Ratio / GSIB / LCR / NSFR / HQLA / RWA / UMR, cause banks to actively manage their balance sheet accordingly in order to optimize each regulation.

This allows them to increase balance sheet efficiency and reduce/increase exposures where required to manage dynamic regulatory constraints, thus requiring banks to tightly manage scenarios that impact their business.

The varying legal structure of each bank means the impact of measures of regulation causes different weighted balance sheet challenges for each of the banks. It is not one-size-fits-all.

As banks navigate these challenges, it can mean they have diverging strengths, in their product offerings, at various points in the year compared to their competition. Thus, there can be situations whereby banks will be less willing to intermediate trades even if they have the cash or bonds, as they are constrained by the regulations.

2. Liquidity Stress

Simultaneously, liquidity stress can manifest when a surge in demand for cash suddenly spikes, and a contraction in supply is encountered, i.e., lenders become nervous – for example, due to heavy bond issuance or tax deadlines – causing interest rates to surge as participants compete for a dwindling pool of available funding. Additionally, collateral scarcity and sudden spikes in demand for specific bonds can cause pressures.

There have been a number of stress periods over recent years in the financing markets, which have highlighted the need to keep a liquid and functioning collateral market.

Localized liquidity gaps can rapidly evolve into broader market contagion in the outright and ultimately futures markets. Consequently, the accessibility of central bank facilities and connectivity to intermediating technology venues becomes increasingly important as the speed of execution accelerates with technological advances and the market moves to faster, and increasingly automated, execution.

The Role of Central Banks

An efficient, functioning market depends on continuous access to secured funding, and central banks undoubtedly play a pivotal role, both by providing facilities for normal market conditions, and by promoting emergency facilities that play a market stabilizing role in times of stress.

In the U.K., the Bank of England’s Short-Term Repo (STR) and Indexed Long-Term Repo (ILTR) operations have become core to managing balance sheet pressure and liquidity stress. Access to these operations is now far more routine in an environment where Quantitative Tightening (QT) has reduced the amount of excess liquidity in the system. The bank has actively reduced the stigma associated with the operations.

Across major jurisdictions, usage patterns differ, but what matters most is that firms can connect to these tools in a straightforward and operationally efficient way. Even where access exists, technological integration and operational readiness determine how effectively participants can use these central bank facilities, particularly around periods of stress in the market and year-end, when balance sheet availability tightens.

CME Group data shows clear seasonal patterns: March 2025 saw $407 billion in U.S. repo volume – the second-highest daily total on record – while European average daily notional volumes (ADNV) reached €350 billion.

The Evolution of Market Access

Participation in cash market infrastructure – be it central limit order books such as BrokerTec, request-for-quote platforms such as BrokerTec Quote or via clearing – improves access and choice for banks and their clients to manage their funding requirements.

As the U.S. repo market readies itself for mandatory clearing for certain repo transactions, the potential for increased use of clearing in the U.K. and Europe continues to remain an active discussion point. The clearing market in Europe is much more fragmented, adding an additional layer of complexity when considering clearing options or mandates in the future.

Additionally, the market structure of Europe and the U.K. are distinctly different to the U.S. where money markets play a much more significant role. The move toward central clearing for the buy-side is no longer just a regulatory hurdle; it is a liquidity strategy.

Today in the U.K. and Europe, barriers remain to the participation of investment managers in repo market clearing infrastructure. As a consequence, timing of execution and certainty of access differs between dealer-to-dealer and dealer-to-client flows.

The access options from LCH and Eurex differ, with both offering different legal frameworks across sponsored and guaranteed clearing models. Ultimately, the selection of a clearing pathway is dictated by a firm’s membership status and the availability of bank sponsorship, a resource that remains constrained. Harmonizing these access points is essential for managers seeking to unlock the capital efficiencies inherent in netting.

As the clearing mandate in the U.S. increases global discussions on implementation, there is a deliberation on how, if and when the rest of the world will follow suit. A strategic pivot could be underway: market pressure could decouple from balance sheet capacity and intensify around capital constraints. Creating margin efficiencies will be paramount for a bank, but conversely, the amount of margin a bank receives could also decline.

The real unknown is the challenge the market faces in establishing a new paradigm. The participants need to ensure that quick access to movement of collateral can compensate for the reduction in margin received (e.g., the inability to rehypothecate collateral for margin efficiency).

While electronification has addressed many of the operational inefficiencies that once constrained repo trading, the next challenge is to ensure that this access is universal and frictionless, enabling collateral to move freely across the system even as sovereign supply continues to rise, ultimately creating a more resilient market.

Broad, uncomplicated access, rather than jurisdictional differences, will determine how well the market absorbs continued record issuance. Ultimately, in a world awash with government debt, the question is no longer whether liquidity exists – it’s whether everyone who needs it can reach it fast enough to keep the system flowing.

data.title: Allspring Municipal Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The fund underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, for the three-month period that ended December 31, 2025.
  • Duration, curve, and credit were additive while selection detracted.

Market review

Municipal bonds had a strong fourth quarter, posting a positive return of 1.56%. This outpaced most taxable fixed income asset classes. Only U.S. mortgage-backed securities (MBS) delivered a stronger fourth quarter return at 1.71%. Outside of MBS and tax-exempt municipals, the next best-performing fixed income asset classes were taxable municipals (1.37%) and U.S. commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS, 1.34%). While still positive, the 10-year Treasury and U.S. corporates had the lowest fixed income returns for the quarter at 0.83% and 0.84%, respectively.

Within municipal credit tiers, BBB-rated bonds performed the best for the quarter, returning 1.77%. Interestingly, high yield municipal debt was the worst-performing municipal credit sector, though it still posted a positive return of 1.15%. For the year, returns were similar across all investment-grade municipal debt, with AAA- through BBB-rated bonds returning between 4.35% and 4.13%. The outlier for the year was high yield municipal debt, which returned just 2.46%. The high yield municipal sector was weighed down by a high-profile credit that experienced a technical default and was downgraded multiple notches. Despite this underperformance, municipal high yield spreads remain rich relative to historical averages.

The municipal AAA yield curve flattened further in the fourth quarter, continuing the trend that began in September. While most of the curve flattening in the quarter happened in the first three weeks of October, the combined September/October flattening move was enough to completely unwind the steepening that took place in the first eight months of the year. The AAA municipal 2-year/10-year yield curve did move marginally steeper in November and December to finish the year very close to where it started.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) lowered its benchmark interest rate at both its October and December meetings. This combined 50-basis-point (bp; 100 bps equal 1.00%) decrease left the overnight federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75%. With most estimates of the neutral rate somewhere between 2.75% and 3.25%, the market is pricing in just over two more cuts in 2026, which would bring the benchmark rate close to neutral. This bias for lower rates, combined with a more dovish FOMC chair coming in May, leads us to expect the yield curve to steepen again in 2026.

Fund attribution and strategy

Duration—modest contributor

  • We were overall short to the index in October and November and the market rallied, so our overall short duration position detracted as the market rallied. However, this was offset by overweights in the 8-year to 15-year duration buckets, which outperformed.
  • Municipal bond returns were positive for the quarter. October municipal performance was particularly strong, especially given the typical weakness we usually see during the large primary issuance calendar in October. While still positive, November and December returns were more subdued, as some of the October performance pulled forward gains from year-end.
  • As we head into 2026, we are biased toward a shorter duration posture. We are expecting another year of strong inflows, offset by another year of record primary issuance. We believe that attractive valuations relative to other fixed income asset classes, along with still-compelling tax-exempt yields, should drive another year of solid performance. Like 2025, we believe income will be the main driver of returns, as rate volatility could increase with a new FOMC chair.

Yield curve—contributor

  • We were underweight bonds inside of 8 years, which was positive as shorter bonds underperformed. An underweight to bonds from 9–17 years detracted as these maturities outperformed, but this was offset by an overweights to bonds longer than 18 years as longer-dated bonds outperformed.
  • The municipal benchmark curve saw an “operation twist” rewind in October. The front end of the yield curve moved higher in yield, while the long end continued the September rally. The 1-year/30-year AAA municipal curve ended the third quarter at 192 bps and flattened to a low of 153 bps in November. We did see the curve steepen again in December, to close the year at 165 bps.
  • We believe the increase in front-end municipal yields was more technical in nature (accounts were overweight and the sector had become rich), and the new FOMC chair will be supportive for front-end rates (low likelihood of an increase in overnight rates).

For these reasons, we think the bias will be for the yield curve to steepen again in 2026, continuing the trend that started in December.

Quality—modest contributor

  • An overweight to BBB rated bonds was positive, offsetting an underweight to AAA bonds, which outperformed, and an overweight to A rated bonds, which underperformed.
  • Credit spreads remain tight. Despite the rate volatility, the spread between AAA and high yield credits remains near the tightest levels over the past two years, making credit relatively more expensive. Looking ahead, we believe credit fundamentals are well supported by the economy, though the possibility of credit spread widening remains ever present. There are opportunities for more risk-taking investors to explore the high yield space; however, as the recent downgrade of Brightline demonstrates, security selection remains crucial in this segment of the market.

Sector—neutral

  • We continue to be underweight general obligation (GO) bonds and overweight revenue bonds. Controlling for duration, curve, and quality allocation, contributors to relative performance included an overweight allocation to the hospital and transportation sectors and an underweight allocation to the prefunded and state GO sectors. Detractors included overweight allocations to the industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue and special tax sectors and an underweight to local general obligation (GO), housing, education, water and sewer, and leasing sectors.
  • In times of economic uncertainty, GO bonds backed by strong income and sales tax receipts, along with bonds supported by essential services provided by municipalities, are generally more defensive than discretionary sectors. We remain focused on these areas while also seeking opportunities in higher-beta revenue sectors where price dislocations create value. These dynamics underscore the need for an experienced team with institutional bandwidth to navigate an evolving market.

Issue selection—detractor

  • Controlling for duration, curve, quality, and sector allocation effects, security selection was negative for the quarter. Relative to the benchmark, the largest contribution effects were seen in the prefunded, state GO, education, and special tax sectors. Selection within the local GO, electric, housing, hospital, transportation, water and sewer, and leasing sectors detracted.
  • The volatility observed throughout the year serves as a reminder to bond investors that municipals are credit products and not just a rates asset class. Security selection remains critical, as certain credits may diverge from the broader market due to forced selling, liquidity constraints, and structural factors. These inefficiencies can lead to atypical execution and present opportunities for strong risk-adjusted returns among liquidity providers. Prudent liquidity management has been a hallmark of our investment process, enabling us to capitalize on ongoing market dislocations while identifying compelling relative value opportunities.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is an unmanaged index composed of long-term tax-exempt bonds with a minimum credit rating of Baa. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Bond Index tracks the performance of U.S. municipal bonds that are not exempt from federal income tax. This index represents a diversified, investment-grade portion of the U.S. municipal bond market, covering issuers such as state and local governments and their agencies. These bonds are distinct from the more common tax-exempt municipal bonds because they are subject to taxation. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment-grade, U.S.-dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, mortgage-backed securities (agency fixed-rate and hybrid adjustable-rate mortgage pass-throughs), asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Definition of Terms:

30-day SEC yield: The 30-day SEC yield is calculated with a standardized formula mandated by the SEC. The formula is based on maximum offering price per share and includes the effect of any fee waivers. Without waivers, yields would be reduced. The 30-day unsubsidized SEC yield does not reflect waivers in effect. A fund's actual distribution rate will differ from the SEC yield, and any income distributions from the fund may be higher or lower than the SEC yield.

Average maturity: The average maturity represents the weighted average time to maturity of all the debt securities held in the portfolio. A relatively short average maturity results in smaller price fluctuations in response to changes in market rates of interest. A short average maturity subjects the owner of a debt portfolio to the risk that maturing debt will be replaced with debt carrying a lower interest rate. Average maturity is an important consideration for investors who hold bond and money market funds.

Alpha: measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta: measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Credit-quality ratings: Credit-quality ratings apply to underlying holdings of the fund and not the fund itself. data.title: Goldman Sachs U.S. Equity Insights Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

US large cap equities (S& am p;P 500 Index) rose 2.66% in the fourth quarter of 2025. Sector performance was generally positive, led by Health Care and Com munication Services, while Real Estate and Utilities were the only notable laggards. Monetary policy remained a key macro driver, as the Federal Reserve delivered two additional 25 basis point rate cuts in October and December following the initial September cut, reinforcing a more accommodative policy stance. Market sentiment was intermittently challenged by late-year volatility tied to macro data, earnings revisions, and ongoing geopolitical and trade policy uncertainties.

Performance Review

The Goldman Sachs U.S. Equity Insights Fund (I-share, net) returned 3.08% in the fourth quarter, outperforming its benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, by 42 bps. Returns were driven primarily by stock selection, with contributions varying across pillars and sectors as market leadership rotated toward value and cyclicals.

Our Sentiment Analysis pillar was the largest contributor to excess returns during the quarter, driven by notable positive contributions in October and December. Signals designed to capture investor sentiment and short interest performed well after guiding additive overweight positioning in in Aerospace and Defense and Passenger Airlines names within the Industrials sector. Improved sentiment towards these industries was driven by a widespread rotation towards cyclicals amid easing financial conditions.

Our Themes & Trends investment pillar also contributed positively over the quarter. Performance was driven by our suite of signals gauging cross-stock economic linkages, with strength concentrated in areas benefiting from cyclical recovery dynamics and improving demand trends. Industry-level positioning within travel-related segments contributed during December, as demand indicators strengthened toward year-end.

The High-Quality Business Models pillar modestly added to performance as well, supported by management quality and balance sheet-focused signals. Contributions were driven by selective positioning in companies exhibiting resilient earnings profiles and stable cash-flow characteristics, particularly within the Industrials and Consumer Discretionary sectors.

In contrast, Fundamental Mispricings detracted from returns during the quarter. While relative valuation signals contributed positively at times - particularly within Industrials and Materials - these gains were offset by weaker performance from signals seeking to monitor Industry Rotations earlier in the quarter. Sector positioning within Information Technology also detracted, as stock selection within both software and semiconductor segments faced headwinds amid valuation sensitivity and shifting expectations around AI-related capital spending.

data.title: Rubrik: High Growth Rates And AI Resilience
data.description:
While it's undeniable that there is plenty of uncertainty in the air right now amid a shaky macroeconomy and the possibility of a drawn-out conflict in the Middle East, it's also rather plain to see that the selloff, particularly in software stocks, has gone too far. The "SaaSpocalypse" narrative that has hung like a dark cloud over software stocks this year has simply not been reflected in any near-term earnings data, and investors who have a strong stomach for the near term can take advantage of multi-year lows to buy high-quality businesses at a very attractive price.

Rubrik (RBRK), a cybersecurity-adjacent software company that specializes in backup and cyber resilience, is one of these deeply mispriced stocks. Shares are down ~30% since the start of January alone, wiping out all of last year's gains. On the heels of a strong Q4 earnings print and an upbeat outlook for FY27, it's a good time for investors to reassess the bull case in this name.

I last wrote a buy article on Rubrik in January, when the stock was still trading at $75 per share. At the time, I had called out the stock's immense growth rates and its rising free cash flow margin profile, with a note of caution on the company's premium valuation multiples. Since then, the company has continued to deliver upbeat fundamentals, but the "SaaSpocalypse" narrative has taken a huge bite out of its valuation multiples, though there is virtually no concrete evidence that AI poses any threat at all to its business (in fact, the rise of AI agents may even increase the need for its resilience products). I'm reiterating my buy rating here.

Let's kick off the discussion by quickly recapping what Rubrik does and how the AI selloff narrative fails to fit in here. Rubrik began selling off sharply in February, when Anthropic's Claude unveiled new security capabilities that made investors nervous about the entire cybersecurity space.

Rubrik slots in somewhat into the cybersecurity category, but its bread and butter isn't traditional firewalls and threat detection. Instead, Rubrik focuses on data resilience. The company's thesis is that the majority of cyberattacks don't come from hackers breaking into an enterprise system but simply logging in. The company's products focus on how to respond to events like this (while other cyber companies focus on how to prevent them). The company helps its customers back up their identity data and permissions to ensure minimum leakage and enable recovery of data assets in the event of an attack.

The key point that many investors may miss here is that Rubric's product involves a wide array of critical integrations to third-party software. Rubrik connects to firewall-style cybersecurity platforms like Palo Alto Networks (PANW) and Zscaler (ZS), but it also connects to enterprise software platforms like Salesforce (CRM) and data stored inside the likes of Azure and AWS to ensure businesses can remain active during a threat incident. It's difficult to imagine any company self-building this kind of tool using agentic AI or vibe coding and managing integrations to all of these various platforms without risking errors. I consider Rubrik's platform to be quite AI-resistant; in fact, I believe the rise of agentic AI that can perform tasks makes it even more critical that IT departments prepare for unwanted incidents.

Stay long here: none of Rubrik's recent results lend credence to the narrative that AI is a threat to its business.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through Rubrik's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Rubrik's revenue grew 46% y/y to $377.7 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $342.4 million (+33% y/y) by a huge thirteen-point margin. We have to call out upfront that a large chunk of Rubrik's recent revenue growth has been driven by the transition of its "material rights" deals. The company is phasing out selling hardware/software packages, and legacy customers received subscription credits instead of the hardware appliance refreshes they would have received in the past, which was recognized as additional subscription revenue.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases the company's revenue normalized for this one-time tailwind, which added $7 million to Q4 revenue and $18 million to FY26 revenue (the benefit of this transition will fade in FY27, where the company is expecting only $10 million in revenue due to these material rights). We note that adjusted for this one-time revenue, Rubrik's normalized revenue would have grown at a 43% y/y pace, which is seven points stronger than 36% y/y in Q3; in fact, it was the best quarter all year. Again, we emphasize here: it looks like AI is a tailwind rather than a headwind to Rubrik's business.

We note as well that the company added $115 million of net-new subscription ARR within the quarter, growing its subscription ARR by 34% y/y to $1.46 billion.

The company's enlarged subscription base has also led to meaningful economies of scale. Pro forma gross margins rose 400 bps y/y to 83.7%, which skews very high even in the software industry.

Pro forma operating margins, meanwhile, improved to 1.6%. While still early on in its profit development, we note that Rubrik's pro forma operating margin improved 13 points versus -11.2% in the year-ago Q4.

Meanwhile, the company also delivered $237.8 million in free cash flow, rising 11x y/y and hitting an 18% margin that improved 16 points y/y.

Valuation, Risks, and Key Takeaways

At current share prices near $54, Rubrik trades at a $10.81 billion market cap. After we net off the $1.68 billion of cash and $1.13 billion of convertible debt on Rubrik's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $10.26 billion.

For FY27 (the year for Rubrik ending in January 2027), the company is guiding to total revenue of $1.597-$1.607 billion, which represents as-reported revenue growth of 21-22% y/y. The company notes that excluding the tough headwind to higher material rights revenue in FY26, true underlying growth would be 27-28% y/y. Management is also expecting $265-$275 million in FCF, or 17% FCF margins and 14% y/y FCF growth (considering FY26 FCF margin clocked in at 18%, there's likely room for upside here).

Rubrik now trades at:

  • 6.4x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 38x FY27 FCF

For a company expected to deliver just shy of 30% organic growth in FY27 alongside a burgeoning cash flow profile, Rubrik, to me, represents the epitome of "growth at a reasonable price."

Of course, there are also several risk factors that we need to watch out for. Cyber resilience may be a critical product to position in an IT stack, but it isn't as 100% critical as traditional firewalls. Amid a tough macro, IT budget pressures may prevail and push IT leaders to spend more of their budgets on enabling agentic AI operations rather than purchasing safeguards like Rubrik.

That said, we still find more positives than negatives in this stock, especially after this year's sharp slide has given us a more de-risked entry point at an attractive valuation. Buy the dip and stay long.

data.title: PagSeguro Digital: Brazil's Potential Rate Cuts And Credit Expansion Support Re-Rating
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered PagSeguro Digital (PAGS), I highlighted their strong top- and bottom-line growth, ramping-up their double-digit shareholder returns and showing their resilience while growing very well in payments and banking even during the ongoing macro pressures.

Following a solid report seen recently, PAGS remains a Strong Buy, backed by a very attractive valuation, a sustainable double-digit yield, and significant near- and long-term potential thanks to several tailwinds.

Internal Developments

PAGS reported a solid Q4 and 2025 as a whole, with a 16% YoY increase in revenue, of which 9% was in payments and a strong 51% in banking, while the net income grew at a more modest 4%, which is still an excellent performance given the ongoing macro pressure, all being either at the top end of the guidance or even above it if we're looking at the 20.6% EPS growth rate.

PAGS also released their 2026 guidance and confirmed the 2029 ambitions, expecting a very strong 25% to 35% growth rate this year in their credit portfolio, which could reach BRL 25 billion by the end of 2029, while the gross profit and EPS would grow at a CAGR of over 10% and 16% from 2025 to 2029, respectively, despite a slower 2026 due to macro uncertainty.

Financially, based on PAGS's latest report (note that this is in thousands of reais), we continue to see a very strong position, with their cash and cash equivalents doubling YoY to the equivalent of about US$357.7 million, while the current assets continue to cover their total liabilities, being in a very strong position overall, which I believe should allow them to keep expanding in the region.

PAGS returned roughly BRL 2.1 billion back to shareholders in 2025, which represents about 87.5% of their net income, which currently represents a whopping ~14.28% combined yield, which continues to be very strong. The current buyback program is ~80% complete now, and I expect them to renew it soon, likely even during the next meeting, with the company's CFO mentioning during their Q4 Earnings Call that they're likely to deploy the rest over the next months:

So we have the third buyback program that was launched last May. It remains open. we have been executing since then, approximately 80% of this buyback programs have been executed, and we're probably going to deploy the rest of this problem in the upcoming months.

Macro Developments

Probably the biggest macro development seen since comes from the expectations surrounding Brazil's SELIC rate (i.e., the benchmark rate set by the Central Bank of Brazil), with the market now expecting the following 7 policy meetings in 2026 to come with cuts of 50 basis points each after being maintained at the latest meeting.

This way, the SELIC rate would get to 11.5% by the end of 2026 compared to the current 15% rate, which can be amazing for a company like PAGS, which depends on economic activity, transaction volumes, new businesses, etc. The company also talked about this during their Q4 Earnings Call, while estimating it at a level between 12.5% to 13% by the end of 2026 and 10% for 2027:

When we talk about the SELIC rate, that's very important when we talk about the financial cost. When we look and what we expect for 2026, despite that we will face cuts in the interest rate along the year, the average SELIC probably is going to be quite close to the 2025 SELIC rate. And at the same time, we also assume that and included that in our 2026 guidance.

They also mentioned how big the impact of this rate is on their financing costs, which shows another layer of importance for the company, other than the natural slowdown effect it has on the consumer and businesses:

On the cost side, financial costs increased 39% year-over-year, driven mainly by the higher interest rate environment and the effects of recent capital structure adjustments as highlighted earlier. On the other hand, sequentially, financial costs reduced 1% due to the progress we have made in diversifying our funding structure and reducing our funding costs.

Not only that, but I mentioned before that all the ongoing US pressure and uncertainty can even lead to a migration of capital even into assets like this one, as the market could seek to diversify away from the country, while Brazil continues to offer several very high-yield opportunities, including PAGS.

The next SELIC decision is expected on March 18, but these expectations came before the ongoing Iran conflict, so it wouldn't be impossible to see some further delays until the situation potentially normalizes. Since Brazil is also a key oil and commodity player, this conflict can also cause potential disruptions, as the fundamentals of oil remain relatively unstable, with production growth being met by weak supply, both of which can be affected by the current high prices we see today.

This remains an ongoing matter, and even though I don't expect it to last for that long since an actual regime change is likely not the goal, every day spent at these prices can put even more pressure on an already weak global economy that was only recently starting to show some signs of improvement, potentially hurting PAGS' clients and the final consumer.

Risks Reiterated

Although there is potential to see improvements, there's certainly the possibility to see macro pressure continue, and given the ongoing Iran conflict and potential for things to escalate, we could even see delays in Brazil's rate cuts.

Since the company is ultimately tied to economic activity, this has the potential to cost them quite a bit, especially since they base their long-term estimates on lower rates, which remains an unpredictable topic, with potential delays likely resulting in the market "taxing" their valuation.

Valuation

For PAGS' DCF to Equity valuation, starting from their BRL 7.56 billion CFO, minus the BRL 2.28 billion in CAPEX and BRL 2.33 in net borrowings, getting us to an FCFE of roughly BRL 2.96 billion, getting us to a very strong US$570.5 billion in 2025.

Just to be extra conservative from the start and assume a CAGR of 12% until 2029, which is well below the 16% EPS CAGR they expect (they say this doesn't include buybacks, by the way), followed by a CAGR of 8% until year 10 and a 3% terminal growth rate given the region's potential, with a very high 15% discount rate to be extra careful given the region's and industry's risks, with 290.7 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

Despite using conservative assumptions that are well below the company's near-term estimates and potential, we get to an equity value of $7.65 billion, for an intrinsic value of US$26.30 per share, which is significantly above the current US$9.98 at the moment of writing this, leaving a massive margin of safety.

Conclusion

PAGS remains a Strong Buy, backed by significant undervaluation and very strong potential to grow in LATAM, especially if domestic tailwinds advance as expected, with sustainable double-digit returns on top.

There are risks to keep in mind given the competition or even the region itself, but the overall risk-reward remains significantly in their favor, in my opinion, and PAGS remains a position that I consider increasing on a regular basis.

data.title: Arista Networks: Why A Breakout Seems Likely (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in November, I upgraded Arista Networks, Inc. (ANET) from a sell to a hold rating. I remained relatively cautious on the stock, as the business seemed supply constrained and the valuation was deemed to have been fair. As shown in the rating history chart below, Arista Networks has had very mild gains since then. Today, I will evaluate their most recent earnings reported in mid-February as well as assess how some recent developments are affecting their outlook. The goal today is to provide readers with an updated perspective and see if the stock has become a buy in my eyes.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Arista Networks continues to be supply constrained as purchase commitment growth far outpaces revenue increases. While this risk may have played a factor in their 2026 guidance, the company has a massive opportunity at hand, and recent developments show that the likelihood of them capturing a good part of the TAM is high. Therefore, the valuation contraction since my previous article has created a buying opportunity in my view.

TAM Continues To Grow

Before looking specifically at their 2025 Q4 financial results, let's take a big step back and briefly discuss the multiyear opportunity at hand. As you can see in the infographic above, their TAM expanded notably in 2024 from 2023. However, that is a relatively small increase when compared to 2025's annual expansion. TAM is now $105 billion, and while their Core business is taking the spotlight due to the AI boom, their Strategic Expansion and Cognitive Networks businesses also have significant room to grow over the next few years. What investors should take away from this is that the opportunities for Arista Networks are expanding at an accelerating pace. Now, it's likely a matter of execution by management to see how much of the market they can capture.

Growth Ticks Up

For their 2025 Q4 results, let's start with a look at top-line activity and demand trends. In the fourth quarter, Arista Networks generated revenues of $2.5 billion, and that represented a respectable growth rate of 28.9% YoY. This was a mild acceleration from Q3's 27.5% increase, and so there has been a slight increase in business momentum. One of the more encouraging signs for the top line in Q4 was that all three of their product sectors helped to power growth as a sign of broad strength. The company also managed to beat analyst expectations by nearly $100 million, and so, judging by these results, Arista Networks has seen reasonably robust activity as the data center buildout in the U.S. and other countries continues.

For a clear indicator of where demand is, let's take a look at the increase in purchase commitments. QoQ, you can see on the right that there has been a 42% increase. That is a major jump from just the previous quarter and shows that essentially the lineup for Arista Networks' products is getting longer. Note that in QoQ terms, revenues grew by just 7.8%. Therefore, purchase commitment growth is far outpacing the top line. This is usually a clear sign of rock-solid demand. It should also be mentioned here that their deferred revenue grew by 14.6% QoQ, and so overall it seems that supply constraints continue to be a factor that is limiting Arista Networks' growth at this point. This is probably the biggest risk to their business right now.

Margins Are Mixed

With demand being so strong, it was a bit of a surprise for me that their gross margin actually contracted. However, this is probably not a major concern at this point. In their earnings presentation, the company mentioned that Q4's 63.4% is still within their historic range and that "a higher concentration of Cloud & AI Titan sales" is reflected in their gross margin figure. Until there is a more material deterioration that is driven by competitive pressures or substantial cost headwinds, I wouldn't draw the conclusion that profitability is getting eroded.

YoY, Arista Networks' operating expenses increased from $332 million to $397 million as they continued to make strategic investments in areas like liquid cooling and high-radix switching. With innovation being a competitive advantage of theirs, this spending is justified. Furthermore, as a percentage of revenues, operating expenses actually declined from 17.2% to 16%, and that is clearly reflected in the operating margin figure. While gross margin was a bit unfavorable, strong operational efficiency helped to more than offset that, as operating margin actually expanded by 50 basis points. Therefore, core profitability of the company is in a good position currently.

On the bottom line, growth did accelerate for EPS, and they were also able to beat expectations by $0.06. While the 26% increase to $0.82 actually underperformed the top line growth rate to show mixed profitability, I believe investors should mainly focus on the operating margin number above for a clearer picture of where true operating profitability is.

Outlook & Opportunities

The company provided both quarterly and annual guidance. We'll focus on the full year 2026 numbers here, but it should be mentioned that they guided Q1 revenues above expectations. The annual outlook is shown above, and you can see that they raised their guidance for some key metrics. For top line growth, they now see around 25%. This is contrasted with a 28.6% increase for the full year 2025, and so there is some momentum slowdown expected. As stated earlier, the risk of supply constraints is much more noteworthy than demand worries at this point. It seems that this revenue estimate implies that supply will continue to be an issue moving forward.

As for profitability, both gross margin and operating margin are set for contraction relative to 2025 if the targets laid out above are met. Therefore, operating efficiency may not come to the rescue like it did in Q4. Overall, despite their raising of some expectations, I'm a bit disappointed with this near-term outlook, as at the end of the day both business activity and profitability are set to soften from what we saw in the past year.

After their earnings release, there have been some developments that show that the company does have good opportunities and growth drivers. In late February, AMD (AMD) and Meta (META) announced what was described as a megadeal. This is a clear indication that the AI infrastructure buildout is set to continue. With Arista Networks being a key supplier of infrastructure solutions, the company is set to see tailwinds from this deal specifically and from continued capital spending by hyperscalers in general. In my view, this shows that the long-term outlook remains solid.

Another significant development is that just today (Mar 12th), Arista Networks announced their XPO High Density Liquid Cooled Pluggable Optics. I believe this is a demonstration of their innovative capabilities. Chief Architect Andreas Bechtolsheim stated that,

The unprecedented growth in AI fabric bandwidth and the transition to liquid cooling requires a new generation of pluggable optics modules. XPO solves this challenge by providing fundamental improvements in density, cooling capability and reliability for pluggable optics modules.

The release of such technologies shows that the company's commitment to R&D is justified and that they remain in position to capture a good part of the market. As mentioned earlier in the TAM discussion, the opportunity's there, and now it's a matter of execution. I would say that despite the supply constraints and softer than desired 2026 guidance, Arista Networks' positioning is quite strong for the long term.

Valuation A Bit More Attractive

Despite some ups and downs, the forward P/E ratio has come down from the 43 reading seen in my previous article. While the current level of 38.41 for the multiple isn't the cheapest seen in the market today, the potential for high levels of growth over the medium to long term makes the stock attractive.

In today's analysis, we learned quite a bit about where the fundamentals and outlook of Arista Networks are at. Firstly, demand continues to be through the roof as purchase commitments soar. Judging by their Q4 revenue and 2026 guidance, supply remains the top-of-mind issue for the company. Over time, I expect bottlenecks to ease somewhat, and so, given that the company is a leader in AI infrastructure solutions, there is a good chance that they will be able to capture a significant part of the TAM that they outlined. In my view, the company may be dealing with some near-term challenges, but the long-term outlook has continued to strengthen.

In my previous article, I concluded that the valuation was more or less fair. With the long-term prospects further brightening since then and the forward P/E ratio having come down a bit, I believe the stock is now in a position where the risk/reward is favorable for investors.

Given that their revenue for the full year 2025 was around $9 billion but the TAM is now estimated to be above $100 billion, you can see that the potential for growth is immense. As stated above, Arista Networks is in a position to capitalize on the opportunities at hand. Therefore, while the forward P/E of 38 doesn't seem particularly low using forward 1-year analyst estimates, on a multiyear timeframe the multiple would drop to irrationally low levels if the stock price doesn't rise from here.

Final Thoughts

After the November selloff, the stock has been mostly stuck in an extended period of consolidation. I believe there is a good chance that this will end with a new leg up for Arista Networks. The combination of an expanding TAM, innovative products, and a pullback in the valuation sets the stock up for outperformance even though near-term supply challenges persist. As a result, I've decided to upgrade Arista Networks to a buy rating for the first time.

data.title: Petco Health And Wellness Company's Turnaround Has Officially Started
data.description:
March 12th was a fantastic day for shareholders of Petco Health and Wellness Company (WOOF). Shares traded up 34.6% for the day. This surge came after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. After seeing years of revenue declines, as well as mixed financial performance when it came to profits and cash flows, the worst for the business looks like it might be over. Although earnings per share fell short of what analysts were anticipating, revenue exceeded expectations. More importantly, management is pointing toward a potential for revenue growth this year, with overall profitability expected to rise as well.

This is great because, since I last wrote about the business back in March of 2023, the stock has taken a huge dive. Even after this increase in share price, shares are down 68.6%, while the S&P 500 is up 64.9%. Truly, it has been a painful couple of years for investors. But if the worst is over, and when we add on top of this the fact that shares are incredibly cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to similar companies, then upside potential becomes justified. In the near term, I wouldn't be surprised to see its share price pull back. That is common in cases like this. But for those focused on the long haul, this might be the pivotal moment where the turnaround is finally coming to fruition. Given how cheap the stock is, I think that the risk profile for investors is quite low. Because of this, I believe that rating it a soft and speculative ‘buy’ is the right move here.

Some Great News At Last!

Over the last few years now, the picture for shareholders of Petco Health and Wellness Company has been bleak. As an example of this, we need only look at the chart above. Revenue fell year after year, dropping from $6.26 billion in 2023 to $5.96 billion in 2025. These 2025 figures are new, having just been released by management. According to the company, the overall drop in sales has been driven by soft discretionary spending and the shifting of consumer preferences toward value-centric products caused by broader economic turbulence.

Truly, these have been trying times for the average consumer in the US. The COVID-19 pandemic created significant inflationary pressures. There were other factors as well, such as a surge in spending by both the Donald Trump and Joe Biden administrations. The Federal Reserve ultimately had to hike interest rates. And that made affordability issues even more challenging. And then, just as things were starting to look up, tariffs came into play, serving as a massive regressive tax for the American consumer. The end result of all of this was a plunge in consumer confidence levels. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean here. The latest jobs data is also discouraging, pointing toward a very likely recession. And unfortunately, even though many pet owners like myself view their pets as members of their family, they aren't afraid to make certain cost cuts when economic times are challenging.

Profitability for the company has been all over the map. Some of this is because of certain one-time events. But regardless, as the first chart in this article illustrates, the overall picture has been rough at best. The good news is that 2025 at least showed some improvement, with every single profitability metric that I looked at for the company showing growth year over year.

The newest data provided by management covered the final quarter of the year. At first glance, it may not look all that impressive. Revenue, as an example, came in at only $1.52 billion. That was down from the $1.55 billion that the company reported a year earlier. Most of this drop was because of a 1.6% decline in comparable sales. The good news is that a lot of this decline was driven, according to the company, by management's decision to move away from unprofitable sales and to also shutter underperforming locations. Even though revenue did drop, it did come in about $1.8 million above what analysts were hoping to see.

On the bottom line, the picture was more nuanced. The company saw its loss per share decline from $0.05 to $0.01. That still resulted in management missing expectations to the tune of $0.02. But at least it still meant an improvement in the bottom line from a net loss of $13.8 million to a net loss of $2.6 million. Clearly, cutting out unprofitable sales and closing underperforming stores is working for the company. And the end result has been not just higher profits but higher cash flows as well. Operating cash flow jumped from $96 million to $153.6 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get an improvement from $91.5 million to $168 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company rose from $96.1 million to $106.3 million.

This is fine and dandy. But the real story here is what management expects moving forward. The firm is pushing its ‘Reach for the Sky’ strategy, which it defines as the third phase of its turnaround plan. You see, after strengthening its operating and economic model and completing the buildout of its senior leadership team with seasoned retail executives, and after focusing on merchandising excellence, improving the customer experience, achieving operational efficiencies, and boosting productivity for the services that they offer, the company is now on what it describes as the offensive. This is centered around long-term revenue growth and profit increases. This seems to be dedicated to improving the company's competitive position, driving operational improvements at its locations, expanding its online experiences, and making additional investments aimed at making its services top of the line.

Management highlighted four pillars of their current economic model. The first of these is to offer a regular stream of product newness that, when combined with its evergreen assortment of offerings, keeps it at the forefront of customers’ awareness. It involves creating memorable and differentiated in-store experiences for its customers that are centered around championing pet parenthood. Another pillar involves ensuring that it continues to employ a national network of groomers, trainers, veterinarians, and pet professionals. And the final part of this is to enhance its digital capabilities that involve a revamped membership offering, pushing for repeat orders and deliveries, and creating what management described as a seamless digital-to-store engagement.

Using these pillars, the company intends to return to growth. But it's not just any growth. It's profitable growth. For 2026 as a whole, it is possible that revenue could be flat. However, the higher end of the revenue range is a sales increase of 1.5%. Even more impressive is the expectation that EBITDA should grow from $408.2 million last year to between $415 million and $430 million this year. If management achieves this, it would mean that the company has successfully grown cash flows two years in a row now. And in all likelihood, hitting the midpoint of the target for EBITDA would mean an increase in adjusted operating cash flow from $240.2 million last year to about $297.5 million this year.

With these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of Petco Health and Wellness Company are valued right now. Honestly, these are very low multiples. Leading up to this point, they reflected a company that seemed to be in a perpetual state of decline. But that is no longer the case if management is correct about the future that the company offers. In that same chart, I compared it to five other pet-oriented businesses. What I found was that, on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group. Meanwhile, on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, only one such firm was cheaper than it is.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's currently available, it is my assertion that Petco Health and Wellness Company serves as a compelling, if still risky, prospect. I actually believe that 2026 will see the US enter into a recession. And if I am correct, I believe that the guidance provided by management will end up being revised lower. But as long as we don't see a collapse in fundamentals, and as long as the nuts and bolts are there to ensure long-term success, I believe that the picture is attractive enough to justify rating it a soft and speculative ‘buy.’
